title: Congressional Ball

jokes.text: Would you like to keep the mugs for your... minivan?
jokes.setup: Selina is schmoozing Congresswoman Nickerson with White House gift mugs
jokes.punchline: The pause before 'minivan' reveals Selina's barely-concealed contempt for the suburban mom congresswoman — she's weaponizing a compliment as a class condescension
jokes.explanation: The ellipsis before 'minivan' does a lot of work — it signals Selina's internal condescension while maintaining plausible deniability. Classic Veep character comedy where the insult is almost polite. Strong character integration because only Selina would weaponize a gift mug this way.

jokes.text: I mean, my license expired a few years ago, but I get the general idea.
jokes.setup: Nickerson bonds with Selina over driving a minivan and says 'you know the drill'
jokes.punchline: Selina's admission that she hasn't driven in years but pretends to relate anyway — pure political fakery exposed
jokes.explanation: Efficiently exposes Selina's fakeness — she's pretending to relate to a stay-at-home mom while admitting she doesn't even drive. The understatement 'but I get the general idea' is the engine. Economical and character-specific.

jokes.text: Okey-dokey, Annie Oakley. Bless your pea-picking heart.
jokes.setup: Selina has just pitched the amphibious fighting boat deal to Nickerson
jokes.punchline: Nickerson responds with an almost satirically folksy, Southern-grandmother register that signals 'yes' in the most politically evasive way possible
jokes.explanation: The folksy phrase is a running joke that pays off later in the episode. As a standalone it lands on the absurdity of congressional politeness as evasion, and 'bless your pea-picking heart' is a genuine Southern put-down delivered as saccharine agreement. Sets up a great callback.

jokes.text: My Donald. He has chemo today. (GASPS) Fantastic.
jokes.setup: Selina offers to let Nickerson call someone from Air Force One to impress them; Nickerson says her husband has chemo today
jokes.punchline: Selina's reflexive 'Fantastic' in response to hearing about someone's chemotherapy — pure autopilot politician mode
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional. The structure is perfect: offer a fun perk → unexpected dark reveal → Selina's brain hasn't caught up to the words leaving her mouth. 'Fantastic' is a single word doing enormous work. It's quintessential Selina — so locked into schmoozing mode she enthusiastically endorses cancer treatment. One of the episode's best moments. Economy is essentially flawless; a lesser show would add a recovery line. Veep just cuts to theme music.

jokes.text: Let me tell you something else about Selina Meyer, our commander-in-thief. Check out the tits in the third row. I could dribble those things like basketballs.
jokes.setup: A political opponent is railing against Selina Meyer at a rally
jokes.punchline: Mid-speech, he breaks to lewdly comment on an audience member's breasts — revealing him as a hypocrite misogynist
jokes.explanation: Works as a satirical jab at political hypocrisy — the man attacking a female president objectifies women in the same breath. 'Commander-in-thief' is a decent wordplay. The basketball dribbling simile is grotesque enough to land as dark comedy. Not exceptional but purposeful.

jokes.text: My name is Jonah Ryan and I... CROWD: Won't back down!
jokes.setup: Jonah is doing a campaign rally, apparently having adopted Tom Petty's 'Won't Back Down' as his theme song
jokes.punchline: The crowd shouts the Tom Petty lyric — implying Jonah has literally named himself after the song's chorus without understanding the copyright implications (later confirmed by angry lawyers)
jokes.explanation: Funny because it implies Jonah has adopted a song as campaign anthem and the crowd is doing it right — the absurdity is that this is actually working for him. The Tom Petty lawyers detail later amplifies this.

jokes.text: Jim, if our nation's enemies decide to drop the puck on hostilities, then our navy and our allies will be very glad we have the military capabilities we need.
jokes.setup: Reporter asks Mike about the amphibious fighting boat being in Nickerson's district — a classic pork barrel question
jokes.punchline: Mike uses a hockey metaphor — 'drop the puck' — instead of 'drop the ball' or standard military language, revealing he's been coached on hockey talking points
jokes.explanation: This is the first hint of Mike's hockey obsession subplot. Integrating it into a press briefing answer about military policy is elegant — the inappropriateness of sports metaphors in national security contexts is perfectly Veep. Sets up the press conference callback.

jokes.text: Oh, I just heard the buzzer. That is the end of the third period. See you at the next face-off.
jokes.setup: Reporter asks about Catherine; Mike uses the hockey buzzer as an excuse to end the press conference
jokes.punchline: Mike fabricates a hockey ending to the press briefing to dodge the Catherine question — the audacity of inventing a sports clock for government briefings
jokes.explanation: The escalation from one hockey metaphor to completely restructuring a White House press briefing around hockey periods is excellent. 'See you at the next face-off' as a sign-off is genuinely funny writing. Strong character integration — only Mike would do this and somehow think it was seamless.

jokes.text: Well, I suppose putting a few pictures up of Muhammad never hurt anyone.
jokes.setup: Staff informs Selina the White House officially celebrates multiple religious holidays including Mawlid al-Nabi
jokes.punchline: Selina's breezy dismissal — suggesting images of Muhammad at the White House — is spectacularly ignorant of the literal most controversial religious taboo in Islam, delivered as if it's nothing
jokes.explanation: Selina's ignorance is the joke — she's treating Mawlid al-Nabi like Christmas (put up some pictures). The specificity of the error (depictions of Muhammad being the most sensitive possible thing) makes it sharp satire rather than cheap provocation. Pure character comedy — Selina's obliviousness is weaponized.

jokes.text: She's a lesbian, Mike. She's not a werewolf. Although either one would explain why she never shaves her legs.
jokes.setup: Mike mentions press interest about Catherine's 'condition'
jokes.punchline: Selina corrects 'condition' to 'lesbian' with the werewolf comparison, then pivots to a leg-shaving joke — reducing her daughter's sexuality to a grooming punchline
jokes.explanation: Two-part joke with excellent structure. First: the 'she's not a werewolf' correction is already good. Then the pivot — 'although either one would explain why she never shaves her legs' — escalates and lands harder. The joke reveals Selina's character: she's not homophobic, just narcissistic and casually cruel about her daughter. High quotability. One of the episode's better crafted lines.

jokes.text: When does the White House's most useless press secretary list come out? I can't wait to see who's number one this year.
jokes.setup: 'The Hill's' 50 Hottest Staffers list has been mentioned
jokes.punchline: Selina pivots from the hot list to mock Mike's uselessness — the 'can't wait to see number one' implying she already knows it's him
jokes.explanation: Solid Selina cruelty — the implication that Mike winning 'most useless' is a given is cutting. Not the sharpest Veep line but functional. The fake-casual delivery implied by 'I can't wait to see' is the engine.

jokes.text: Every year it means a month of horny Senate aides looking down my blouse.
jokes.setup: The 50 Hottest Staffers list is being discussed and Amy says she hates it
jokes.punchline: Her reason is the specific consequence: horny Senate aides ogling her — stated as fact, not complaint, which makes it darker
jokes.explanation: Amy's matter-of-fact delivery of workplace harassment as a logistical inconvenience is classic Veep — the show treats sexism as ambient background noise that characters navigate rather than confront. Works as character comedy more than pure joke.

jokes.text: Also, their methodology is unsound.
jokes.setup: Amy complains about the hot list for a real reason (harassment); Kent chimes in
jokes.punchline: Kent objects to the hot list on statistical methodology grounds — completely ignoring the harassment concern to critique data quality
jokes.explanation: Four words and it's one of the episode's best jokes. Kent objecting to attractiveness rankings on methodological grounds is perfect character integration — his robotic data-brain can't engage with the human complaint so he redirects to what he can evaluate. The juxtaposition with Amy's harassment concern makes it darker and funnier. Near-perfect economy.

jokes.text: I don't think you're in it this year, Ame. There you go, Amy. Problem solved.
jokes.setup: Amy complains about the harassment the hot list causes her
jokes.punchline: Dan points out she's not on it this year as if that solves her problem — treating her absence from a hot list as good news re: sexual harassment
jokes.explanation: 'Problem solved' is doing the heavy lifting — Dan's casual cruelty in treating 'not hot enough to harass' as a compliment is very Dan. Character-specific and economical. Works in rhythm with Kent's preceding line.

jokes.text: Who's 22? The Elephant Man?
jokes.setup: Everyone is shocked that Gary is ranked 21st hottest
jokes.punchline: Selina implies whoever is ranked 22 must be grotesque — if Gary is 21, the drop-off must be severe
jokes.explanation: Quick, sharp callback. The Elephant Man reference is not the most original insult but the comedic logic is sound — if Gary is 21, 22 must be a monster. Economy is the strength here. Gets a solid laugh in rhythm.

jokes.text: Rose hip tea, okay? Or I'm gonna 86 you.
jokes.setup: Gary has just been revealed as #21 on the hot list; everyone is staring/mocking; Gary asserts himself with a tea threat
jokes.punchline: Gary's response to being a hot list sensation is to threaten to cut off someone's tea access — revealing his power is entirely domestic and servile
jokes.explanation: Gary asserting authority via tea threats is perfect character comedy — his domain is beverages and bags, so that's where his power lives. The phrase '86 you' is borrowed from restaurant culture, which fits Gary's servile world. Character-specific.

jokes.text: The emails from Tom Petty's lawyers keep on getting angrier and angrier.
jokes.setup: Jonah's campaign seems to be going well; Dan gives an update
jokes.punchline: The throwaway reveal that Jonah is using Tom Petty's 'Won't Back Down' without licensing and Tom Petty's lawyers are escalating — stated as a minor campaign update
jokes.explanation: Throwaway escalation that adds a layer to the already-absurd Jonah rally. 'Keep on getting angrier and angrier' implies this has been going on for a while and is just a background catastrophe nobody is addressing. Very economical — one line does the whole job. Feels authentic to how campaigns actually function.

jokes.text: How am I not on 'The Hill's' 50 Hottest Staffers list this year? This year? It's the 50 Hottest Staffers, Jonah, not the 50 people most likely to kill themselves before trial.
jokes.setup: Jonah complains about not being on the hot list
jokes.punchline: Dan's reframe: the list is for attractive people, not the psychologically disturbed and legally endangered — 'before trial' adds unexpected dark specificity
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best insults. The phrase 'before trial' elevates this from a simple ugly joke to something specific and sinister about Jonah's character — it's not just that he's unattractive, he's the kind of person who ends up on trial. Dark, specific, unexpected. The structure (Jonah's petulant setup, Dan's precision-guided response) is textbook. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: I am on a career rocket ship to Mars right now, Dan, except I'm gonna leave Matt Damon there because the guy made potatoes in his own shit like a fucking animal.
jokes.setup: Jonah is defending himself against Dan's insults, asserting his career is soaring
jokes.punchline: The Mars/Matt Damon/The Martian reference — Jonah uses a movie plot to insult a real celebrity as a non-sequitur flex, revealing he has completely misunderstood The Martian's spirit
jokes.explanation: This is gloriously Jonah. The chain of logic — rocket ship → Mars → The Martian → Matt Damon → potato-in-shit insult — is uniquely his brand of unhinged confidence. The phrase 'like a fucking animal' treating survival as character flaw is peak Jonah. Highly character-specific; no one else in television could deliver this line.

jokes.text: The whole point of the 50 Hottest is not to be on it. It's to use it to learn who to fuck. Find a woman who was on it last year, but not this year. I mean, that's like a 'make her pay for dinner' situation.
jokes.setup: Jonah is upset about not being on the hot list
jokes.punchline: Dan reveals his predatory strategy: target women whose social capital has declined — framed as insider wisdom
jokes.explanation: Deeply Dan — his entire worldview is transactional power dynamics. Teaching Jonah to exploit women whose status has dropped is both horrifying and perfectly in character. The 'make her pay for dinner' as a status-differential metric is specific and funny in a dark way. Only Dan could deliver this as career advice.

jokes.text: If you don't look like someone melted Play-Doh all over a flagpole, it does.
jokes.setup: Jonah asks if Dan's manipulation strategy works
jokes.punchline: Dan confirms it works — provided you don't look like Jonah. The Play-Doh/flagpole image is the specific creative insult
jokes.explanation: The simile is the gold standard of Veep insults: specific, visual, unexpected, and physically accurate to Jonah's lanky, ungainly appearance. 'Melted Play-Doh all over a flagpole' captures Jonah's tall, lumpy, pale awkwardness in a single image. Highly quotable. Dan's delivery as a helpful aside rather than a direct attack makes it funnier — it's almost collegial cruelty.

jokes.text: Yeah. Fucking Gary's number 21? What? Well, that just makes a mockery of the very idea of hot rankings.
jokes.setup: The Gary hot list reveal continues to resonate
jokes.punchline: Dan's outrage at Gary's ranking — not at being excluded himself, but at the fundamental epistemological failure the ranking represents
jokes.explanation: Dan caring about the integrity of attractiveness rankings is funny character work. 'Mockery of the very idea of hot rankings' is a good phrase. Works as a running gag beat but not one of the episode's sharper moments.

jokes.text: Well, it makes sense. He's got beautiful eyes.
jokes.setup: Everyone is baffled by Gary's hot ranking; Selina arrives
jokes.punchline: Selina, without missing a beat, defends Gary's ranking by sincerely noting his eyes — weirdly intimate knowledge about her bag man
jokes.explanation: The sincerity is the joke. Selina validating Gary's attractiveness with genuine familiarity ('beautiful eyes') reveals the bizarre codependent intimacy of their relationship in two words. Perfect economy and strong character integration — Selina knows Gary's face better than she knows her own daughter's girlfriend's name.

jokes.text: ...squirt a half gallon of ropey jism into their modeled congressional cornholes.
jokes.setup: Roger is given the floor to explain his role in congressional vote-whipping at the White House party
jokes.punchline: After a reasonable tactical breakdown by others, Roger describes his role with a graphic sexual metaphor for political persuasion — exactly what Selina told him not to do
jokes.explanation: Roger is established as the crudely effective operative and this confirms it. The specificity — 'half gallon of ropey jism,' 'modeled congressional cornholes' — is Veep's signature move: the insult so detailed it loops from offensive to absurd. The callback to Selina's 'keep it clean' instruction makes it structurally complete.

jokes.text: No, Roger took my thing about ropey jism.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Tom if he has anything to add to the briefing
jokes.punchline: Tom, the polished, honorable VP candidate, complains that Roger stole his vulgar contribution — revealing he had his own ropey jism talking point ready
jokes.explanation: Exceptional joke. The subversion is complete: Tom James, established as the dignified, principled foil, turns out to have his own filthy version of the same metaphor ready. The deadpan complaint — 'Roger took my thing' — treats the crude sexual language as a professional resource that's been unfairly appropriated. Near-perfect economy. One of the episode's standout moments.

jokes.text: Dirty. Just dirty and shady. Door. Door.
jokes.setup: Gary has witnessed something suspicious about Tom
jokes.punchline: Selina's staccato assessment — 'Dirty. Just dirty and shady.' followed by 'Door. Door.' commanding Gary to manage the door like a dog command — her crisis mode involves issuing single-word orders
jokes.explanation: The Gary-as-obedient-pet dynamic is well-established in Veep and 'Door. Door.' plays it well. The staccato rhythm is Selina at her most pressured. Not exceptional but efficiently character-specific.

jokes.text: But what could O'Brien offer him that's better than the vice presidency? [beat] Literally anything.
jokes.setup: Selina wonders what O'Brien could offer Tom that would flip him
jokes.punchline: Gary's flat 'Literally anything' — acknowledging that working for Selina is categorically worse than any alternative
jokes.explanation: Gary's 'Literally anything' is one of the funniest lines from his character — the one who loves Selina most is also the one who most honestly acknowledges how terrible it is to work for her. The beat/pause before the response is crucial. Economically devastating.

jokes.text: Oh! Tom wanted Secretary of the Treasury. I said no. Ah, fuck!
jokes.setup: Selina realizes Tom may be flipping to O'Brien
jokes.punchline: The realization hits in real time — she remembers she said no to Treasury, immediately understands her mistake, and the 'Ah, fuck!' is the sound of a presidency slipping away
jokes.explanation: The timing of the self-incrimination works well — the scene of discovery and the moment of dawning realization compressed into three sentences. Selina's 'Ah, fuck!' is earned and characterful. The comedy comes from watching someone in real time understand their own catastrophic short-sightedness.

jokes.text: Yes? [I was just checking to see if there was another meeting about me.] Not today.
jokes.setup: Tom enters and Selina's team has just been discussing his treachery
jokes.punchline: Tom calmly asks if they're meeting about him — not defensive, almost curious — and gets the breezy 'Not today,' which implies there are many other days when they do
jokes.explanation: 'Not today' is the perfect answer — technically true, absolutely not reassuring, and reveals that yes, there are many other days. Tom's casual confidence in asking at all is equally funny. Strong economy and character work from both sides.

jokes.text: Well, as the late, great Lionel Richie once said, 'Oh, what a feeling, I am dancing on the debt ceiling.'
jokes.setup: Jonah is asked if he'd vote to raise the debt ceiling
jokes.punchline: Jonah misattributes the quote to 'the late, great Lionel Richie' (who is alive) and conflates 'Dancing on the Ceiling' with the debt ceiling — the pun is bad, the attribution is wrong, and he has no idea
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered stupidity: (1) Richie is alive, (2) the pun is terrible, (3) Jonah is proud of this as a 'zinger.' The fact that Dan immediately calls out both errors ('Lionel Richie is not dead, and what the fuck does that even mean?') amplifies it. Character-perfectly Jonah — his political career is built on exactly this brand of confident, wrong, bad-pun energy.

jokes.text: OKAY, A: Lionel Richie is not dead, AND B: what the fuck does that even mean?
jokes.setup: Jonah delivers his 'late, great Lionel Richie / debt ceiling' zinger
jokes.punchline: Dan's enumerated outrage — he can't even get to the policy problem because the basic facts are wrong first
jokes.explanation: The straight-man reaction to the Jonah absurdity. 'OKAY, A:' as a debate-structure response to campaign trail insanity is funny. Works in tandem with joke 32 — the two are a set.

jokes.text: New Hampshire loves my zingers. It's my personality that has gotten us this far. No, I am the one who got us this far, you sentient enema.
jokes.setup: Jonah credits his personality for the campaign's success
jokes.punchline: Dan's correction ends with 'you sentient enema' — a Veep-grade insult implying Jonah is both waste and a mechanism for removing it
jokes.explanation: 'Sentient enema' is exceptional Veep insult craft. It implies Jonah is (a) waste-adjacent, (b) irritating and invasive, and (c) the mechanism by which other waste is expelled — suggesting the campaign is clearing bad politics by using Jonah. The word 'sentient' adds pseudo-intellectual dignity that makes it land harder. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Mother Mary dry humping a pillow. That is what I call natural talent.
jokes.setup: Judy Sherman, the rival candidate, gives a perfect policy answer about the debt ceiling using a grocery budget analogy
jokes.punchline: Dan's blasphemous exclamation of impressed shock — 'Mother Mary dry humping a pillow' — followed by genuine admiration for her political skill
jokes.explanation: Dan's profane religious exclamation as a compliment is peak character — his admiration for political skill expressed in the most irreverent way possible. The specific image of Mary dry humping is aggressively absurd. High quotability even if the joke is primarily a creative oath.

jokes.text: I'd have to agree with Mrs. Sherman here on fiscal responsibility. In fact, teacher, allow me to present you with this apple.
jokes.setup: Richard is playing Jonah in mock debate practice; he's been instructed to play Jonah but is naturally agreeable
jokes.punchline: Richard-as-Jonah agrees with the opponent and offers a teacher's apple — the opposite of Jonah's combative style, showing Richard cannot play anyone but his gentle self
jokes.explanation: Richard's character — terminally polite and earnest — makes him constitutionally incapable of playing Jonah's aggression. The teacher's apple is the perfect Richardian touch. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Feel my dick. I am rock-hard for you right now, mister. You think I'm kidding. Feel it.
jokes.setup: Richard-as-Jonah has just given a polite, teacher's-apple response; Jonah intervenes to show how he'd really handle it
jokes.punchline: Jonah's version of debate prep involves demanding the debate coach feel his erection as proof of enthusiasm — escalating past any reasonable limit and then escalating again with 'You think I'm kidding. Feel it.'
jokes.explanation: The escalation to 'You think I'm kidding. Feel it.' is the key move — Jonah's sincerity makes it worse. This is cringe comedy at Veep's level: the joke is that Jonah believes this is appropriate debate coaching and won't let it go. Character integration is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Granted, every town up here is just two dirty piles of snow connected by a covered bridge, but Jesus Christ, Jonah, you grew up here. How do you not know this shit?
jokes.setup: Jonah listed Brattleboro, VT as a New Hampshire town at a rally
jokes.punchline: Dan's New England geography dismissal — 'two dirty piles of snow connected by a covered bridge' — conceding his own ignorance of the region while condemning Jonah for his
jokes.explanation: 'Two dirty piles of snow connected by a covered bridge' is an excellent image — specific, contemptuous, and accurate enough to be funny. The self-undermining admission ('granted, I also barely know this') makes Dan's outrage hypocritical, which is funny. Strong economy.

jokes.text: I don't know. Maybe I was supposed to learn it in the second grade, but my teacher was a fucking bitch.
jokes.setup: Dan asks how Jonah doesn't know Vermont from New Hampshire when he grew up there
jokes.punchline: Jonah blames a second-grade teacher for his geographical ignorance — this is both his actual logic and a window into his entire life philosophy
jokes.explanation: Jonah's entire life arc — victim mentality, inability to take responsibility, blaming authority figures — compressed into one sentence about a second-grade teacher. The casual 'fucking bitch' about an elementary school teacher is also funny in its disproportionality. Highly Jonah.

jokes.text: Richard, who do we have tracking the widow? Nobody. Then go out there and buy a camera and videotape everything she does. Do you want 4k or 1080p? Just buy any fucking camera.
jokes.setup: Dan orders Richard to surveil their political opponent with a camera
jokes.punchline: Richard, given a clandestine surveillance mission, asks a consumer electronics question — '4K or 1080p?' — as if buying a birthday present
jokes.explanation: Richard's utter inability to register the gravity or the shadiness of a situation is his defining comic trait, and '4K or 1080p?' is its perfect expression. He's been assigned to secretly film a political opponent and his brain goes to Best Buy. The gap between what's being asked and Richard's response is enormous. Excellent character integration.

jokes.text: If you were like 10% less black, I could make you president.
jokes.setup: Dan and his Black campaign operative are discussing Richard's candidacy potential
jokes.punchline: Dan tells his Black colleague he could be president if he were '10% less black' — a horrifyingly casual statement delivered as a compliment
jokes.explanation: Veep at its darkest satirical: Dan's racism is so casually embedded in his political calculus that he delivers this as a compliment and means it. The recipient's 'Thank you' completes the joke — the political reality they both understand makes this exchange more uncomfortable than funny, which is intentional. Sharp but not cheap.

jokes.text: No. No. No. That's... get it out. We talked about this. The wife of Congressman Platt designed it. Does she know I'm not the president of Cuba?
jokes.setup: Selina arrives somewhere and rejects a decorative item or piece of decor
jokes.punchline: The 'president of Cuba' line suggests whatever decoration has been put up is so garish/politically inappropriate that it could only belong in revolutionary decor — a dig at the Congressman's wife's taste
jokes.explanation: The Cuba reference is funny without seeing the décor — you can infer something overdone and possibly with excessive red/patriotic or tropical motifs. Works as visual comedy inferred from the dialogue. 'President of Cuba' is an unexpected specific reference.

jokes.text: Are you wearing cologne? A little. (SNIFFS) Smells like birdseed.
jokes.setup: Selina notices Gary is wearing cologne — unusual for him
jokes.punchline: 'Smells like birdseed' — Selina's specific, odd olfactory assessment implying Gary's cologne is cheap, outdoorsy, and somehow birdlike
jokes.explanation: 'Birdseed' is an inspired specific insult — not 'cheap' or 'bad' but birdseed, implying Gary has the grooming aspirations of a backyard wildlife feeder. The pause and sniff are implied in the structure. Selina's casual dismissal of Gary's romantic effort is character-perfect.

jokes.text: Do you want to just play it like Ellen or do you want to be more like Jodie Foster? Do you want to play it more like a mystery?
jokes.setup: Selina asks Catherine and Marjorie how they want to handle press questions about their relationship
jokes.punchline: Selina frames her daughter's sexuality as a PR strategy choice between celebrity models — reducing a personal identity question to brand management options
jokes.explanation: Classic Selina — her daughter's coming out is primarily a communications crisis. The specific celebrity templates (Ellen = full open, Jodie Foster = years of mystery) show she's thought about this as a media professional, not a mother. The 'mystery' option is the funniest because it's so cynical.

jokes.text: Okay, can you just speak English, Catherine? 'Cause I don't know what you're trying to say... We'll keep it under the gaydar.
jokes.setup: Catherine says she and Marjorie want to keep things 'on the down-low'
jokes.punchline: Selina pretends she doesn't understand 'down-low' (or genuinely doesn't) then produces 'under the gaydar' — a pun combining 'under the radar' with 'gay' — delivered earnestly
jokes.explanation: 'Gaydar' exists as a word already, but using it as 'under the gaydar' (parallel to 'under the radar') is Selina's own oblivious contribution. She says it without registering the slight, which makes it funnier. The prior 'speak English' complaint about 'down-low' shows her selective slang comprehension.

jokes.text: The fluffers have done their work. It's time for the orgy to begin.
jokes.setup: The congressional party is about to start and Roger signals it's time to begin vote-whipping
jokes.punchline: Roger describes congressional schmoozing as a porn shoot — 'fluffers' (those who warm up performers) and 'orgy' as metaphors for political persuasion
jokes.explanation: Consistent with Roger's pornographic vocabulary for political process. The 'orgy' metaphor for congressional vote-gathering is apt — everyone doing something shameful together. Character-specific but not as sharp as his earlier 'ropey jism' contribution.

jokes.text: You mean 11:00? Over there? Seriously, can you really not tell time?
jokes.setup: An aide tells Selina that Tom James is at 8:00 (as in the clock position)
jokes.punchline: Selina misunderstands '8:00' as a time rather than a directional position — her aide has to clarify 'over there,' and then mocks her inability to interpret a basic spatial convention
jokes.explanation: A quick comedic beat on Selina's occasional obliviousness. The aide's 'can you really not tell time?' is a good burn but the joke itself is fairly standard 'boss doesn't understand military convention' territory.

jokes.text: Except it's still just a rat's nest in the back. It needs to pull... maybe you can help her, Marjorie. Just pull it back, sweetheart.
jokes.setup: Selina praises Catherine's hair but then undercuts it
jokes.punchline: After a faint compliment, Selina tells her daughter's girlfriend to fix Catherine's hair — both dismissing Catherine and casually deploying Marjorie as domestic staff
jokes.explanation: Selina's casual cruelty toward Catherine is a running character note. Using Marjorie as a hair-fixer rather than acknowledging her as a person her daughter loves is the specific comedic violation. Cringe rather than laugh-out-loud.

jokes.text: This is one of my staffers, Nadia. She's a prostitute.
jokes.setup: Selina is meeting Congressman Jaeger at the party; the woman with him isn't his wife
jokes.punchline: The woman accompanying Jaeger is identified as his 'staffer' — then a bystander flatly clarifies 'She's a prostitute' — the deadpan delivery of something that should be scandalous
jokes.explanation: The deadpan 'She's a prostitute' from a bystander is great economy — no elaboration needed. The joke is that everyone at the White House holiday party accepts this as an ambient fact. Selina's seamless pivot to 'Would you like a picture for your friends and family?' ignoring the information is the second punch.

jokes.text: Would you like a picture for your friends and for your family? [congressmen with escort at White House Christmas party]
jokes.setup: Selina has just been informed Jaeger's companion is a prostitute
jokes.punchline: Selina immediately offers a White House photo — obliviously including the prostitute in the official presidential portrait offer
jokes.explanation: Selina's autopilot schmoozing mode continues through the revelation of a sex worker at the White House Christmas party. The specificity of 'for your friends and for your family' — the very people who definitely shouldn't see this photo — makes it funnier.

jokes.text: And you're out of here. You can go.
jokes.setup: Selina is told Congressman Baines is 'solidly O'Brien' — voting against her
jokes.punchline: Selina takes the photo anyway (implied) and then dismisses him with 'you're out of here' as if he's been cut from a sports roster
jokes.explanation: Efficient political comedy — Selina's vote-counting reduces congressmen to assets or liabilities instantly. 'You can go' as presidential dismissal is funny in its bluntness.

jokes.text: Catherine's 23 years old. She's fair game. Maybe you ride the pine on this one for a period or two.
jokes.setup: Mike is being consulted about Catherine's lesbian relationship and how to handle press coverage
jokes.punchline: Mike gives press strategy advice using hockey metaphors — 'ride the pine' (bench-sitting) and 'period or two'
jokes.explanation: The hockey metaphor running gag continues. 'Ride the pine' is appropriate as a real phrase but 'for a period or two' as time measurement in personal life advice is the absurdist escalation. Consistent with Mike's established behavior.

jokes.text: Mike, are you dating a hockey player by any chance? You noticed the hockey references? Shit, I thought they were seamless.
jokes.setup: Selina picks up on Mike's string of hockey metaphors
jokes.punchline: Mike's horrified 'shit, I thought they were seamless' — he genuinely believed no one had noticed his completely obvious hockey fixation
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the hockey running gag is 'shit, I thought they were seamless' — the joke is Mike's complete lack of self-awareness. He has been using hockey metaphors in a White House press briefing and in personal conversations and believed no one noticed. This is the running gag's peak moment. 'Seamless' is the perfect word choice.

jokes.text: Listen, you can't say a word, but our surrogate is pregnant and I've been talking to somebody in the NHL about a job as director of communications. So win or lose, I'm out of here.
jokes.setup: Selina has asked Mike about the hockey player; he confesses the full situation
jokes.punchline: Mike reveals his exit plan mid-episode — the hockey job explains all the metaphors AND he's leaving regardless of the election outcome, making his current job performance even more irrelevant
jokes.explanation: The revelation that the hockey metaphors have a source (Mike is pursuing an NHL job) retroactively makes all the earlier jokes funnier. 'So win or lose, I'm out of here' is both a personal betrayal and a funny indictment of his level of commitment to the current job.

jokes.text: Well, let me get my good-byes out of the way now.
jokes.setup: Mike reveals he's leaving for an NHL job win or lose
jokes.punchline: Selina's immediate response: saying goodbye now, accepting his departure immediately without protest — implying she doesn't value him enough to fight for his loyalty
jokes.explanation: Perfect Selina economy — she doesn't beg, doesn't argue, just pivots to goodbye. The cruelty of the immediate acceptance is the joke. Very character-specific: Selina values loyalty only when it serves her, and Mike's announcement means he's already dead to her.

jokes.text: Ah, Buddy Calhoun in a tie that's not made out of string.
jokes.setup: Selina greets Nevada Congressman Buddy Calhoun at the party
jokes.punchline: The backhanded greeting implies Buddy normally wears ties made of string — commenting on his usual lack of formality while framing the insult as a compliment
jokes.explanation: Economical Selina greeting that does character work on both Selina (always with the condescension) and Buddy (establishing his usual scruffiness). 'Not made out of string' is a good specific image.

jokes.text: You're the only woman who's ever threatened to grate my balls over a bowl of spaghetti. Night's still young. Knock yourself out.
jokes.setup: Buddy and Selina are reminiscing/flirting in their established antagonistic style
jokes.punchline: Buddy frames the ball-grating threat as a compliment/fond memory; Selina's 'night's still young' keeps the threat alive as flirtation
jokes.explanation: The dynamic between Selina and Buddy — threat as flirtation — is established quickly and efficiently. 'Knock yourself out' as an invitation to potentially grate his testicles is exactly Selina's brand of aggressive charm. Economical and specific.

jokes.text: But can you remind me exactly what happened in Bozeman on 9/11? This is mainly focused on our first responders. Our first responders were terribly traumatized by what happened to the first responders in New York.
jokes.setup: A congressman asks Selina to support a 9/11 memorial in Bozeman, Montana
jokes.punchline: Selina's 'what happened in Bozeman on 9/11?' exposes the pork barrel absurdity; the congressman's answer — 'our first responders were traumatized by what happened to the first responders in New York' — is a masterpiece of circular secondary-trauma logic
jokes.explanation: The congressman's logic — Montana first responders traumatized by the trauma of New York first responders — is brilliant political satire. It captures how any project gets justified through 9/11 regardless of proximity or relevance, while the congressman doesn't realize how absurd the reasoning is. Selina's 'Wow. Yeah, Ben' is the correct straight-man reaction. One of the sharper satirical moments in the episode.

jokes.text: Oh, I'm sure we can just slip it under the skirt of the Homeland Security budget. Tell him why, Will. The budget has a fatter, more womanly behind than myself.
jokes.setup: Roger tells the congressman they can hide the Bozeman 9/11 memorial in the Homeland Security budget
jokes.punchline: Roger tells Will to explain; Will provides a bizarre metaphor about the budget having a 'fatter, more womanly behind' — a confusing anatomical compliment to the federal budget
jokes.explanation: Will is a minor character whose function is to deliver absurd elaborations on Roger's crude framings. The Homeland Security budget having a 'fatter, more womanly behind' as a description of its fiscal capacity is delightfully strange. The visual of Will earnestly explaining federal budget anatomy is the source of the humor.

jokes.text: Roger on budget: 'slip it under the skirt of the Homeland Security budget.' Will: 'The budget has a fatter, more womanly behind than myself.'
jokes.setup: Roger suggests hiding the Bozeman 9/11 memorial in the Homeland Security budget
jokes.punchline: Will's elaboration personifies the budget as having 'a fatter, more womanly behind' than himself — a strange, specific, and committed metaphor
jokes.explanation: Will's earnest dedication to extending Roger's skirt metaphor to its logical endpoint is very Veep — no one blinks, they're all too deep in bureaucratic-speak.

jokes.text: Excuse me. Aren't you Gary Walsh? [congressional fan approaches Gary after hot list ranking] What did I do?
jokes.setup: Gary is approached by a starstruck congressional aide's parent who recognized him from the hot list
jokes.punchline: Gary's panicked 'What did I do?' — his first instinct upon being recognized is guilt rather than celebrity. His entire relationship to attention is defensive.
jokes.explanation: Gary's 'What did I do?' is the perfect response for his character — someone so defined by servility and mild self-loathing that celebrity reads as accusation. It's a tiny reaction beat that says everything about Gary. Near-perfect economy and character integration.

jokes.text: She's single, divorced, has a kid. But he's really a sweetheart. [father trying to set Gary up] God, I feel like somebody's playing a trick on him. They're about to dump a bucket of pig's blood on his head.
jokes.setup: Gary is being set up with a congressional father's daughter after the hot list ranking; Selina watches
jokes.punchline: Selina references Carrie — 'dump a bucket of pig's blood on his head' — suggesting that whatever good thing is happening to Gary must be a cruel prank, because only cruelty attaches to Gary
jokes.explanation: The Carrie reference works perfectly — it's how Selina experiences the world, not just Gary's situation. She's so accustomed to bad things happening to Gary that his minor success reads as setup for humiliation. The cultural reference lands because it fits the established cruelty dynamic.

jokes.text: Can't believe how many compliments I'm getting about the hot list. [Amy getting compliments she doesn't want] Don't people around here have anything better to talk about than the stupid hot list?
jokes.setup: Amy complained earlier about the harassment from the hot list; now she's getting compliments — but not being on it
jokes.punchline: Amy is getting compliments not because she's on the list but apparently because people are glad she's not — the irony of compliments going the wrong direction
jokes.explanation: The irony is that the compliments Amy's getting are presumably about NOT being on the list — or the compliments are weirdly off-base. Works as a follow-up to the hot list subplot but not particularly sharp.

jokes.text: Listen, don't worry so much about what's up here, okay? Worry about what's in here. And if you work at that, I'm telling you, you're gonna be back on the hot list, Amy. I'm not saying you're gonna be in the top 20, but you would be a lock for, like, mid 40s.
jokes.setup: Dan approaches Amy with what sounds like genuine emotional support about the hot list
jokes.punchline: Dan's pep talk pivots from inner-beauty platitude to specific hot-list ranking prediction — 'mid 40s' — making it clear his 'what's in here' was also a calculation about her eventual ranking rebound
jokes.explanation: Expertly structured. The setup makes you think Dan is having a genuine human moment, then 'mid 40s' collapses it. The specificity of 'not top 20 but mid 40s' shows Dan has been calculating this — the emotional support was always the setup for a ranking prediction. It's exactly how Dan relates to people, reducing everything to metrics. One of Dan's best moments in the episode.

jokes.text: Actually, she's the only member of the Gay and Lesbian Caucus.
jokes.setup: Connie DiBenedetto is introduced as the 'chairman of the Gay and Lesbian Caucus'
jokes.punchline: An aide clarifies she's also the only member — the chairmanship is self-appointed by default
jokes.explanation: The one-liner deflation of the caucus chairmanship is good economy — no elaboration needed. A committee of one is an oxymoron; being chairman of it even more so. Quick, economical, observational.

jokes.text: And this is her special friend Marjorie... Let's all say girl power.
jokes.setup: Selina introduces Catherine and Marjorie to the Gay and Lesbian Caucus chair while Catherine has just said she doesn't want photos or politicization
jokes.punchline: Selina uses 'special friend' (archaic euphemism for lesbian partner) and immediately manufactures a photo op with the Gay Caucus chair saying 'girl power' — directly violating everything Catherine asked for
jokes.explanation: Selina's 'special friend' is a generational tell (she can't say girlfriend) and immediately deploying Catherine for a gay caucus photo op is the perfect betrayal — Selina agreed to be discreet and then violated it within minutes for political capital. 'Girl power' is the cherry on top.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, I got it. I think I got it! You know, I think I see what happened there. Richard, you good boy. I went to turn it on, but it was already on, so I turned it off. Then I went to turn it off, but it already was off, so I turned it on.
jokes.setup: Richard is trying to explain his camera operation failure — the footage of him getting caught was supposed to be secret footage of the widow
jokes.punchline: Richard's camera logic — a paradox of on/off confusion so pure it loops back to profound — is delivered with total earnestness and concluded with a self-congratulatory 'you good boy' from Dan
jokes.explanation: Richard's camera logic is a genuine comedic achievement: 'I went to turn it on but it was already on so I turned it off. Then I went to turn it off but it already was off so I turned it on.' This is the Veep absurdist structure at its purest — the explanation is coherent, follows internal logic, and results in total chaos. Dan's 'I had such plans for you, Richard' is the perfect defeated response.

jokes.text: I had such plans for you, Richard.
jokes.setup: Richard has explained his camera confusion in complete detail
jokes.punchline: Dan's wistful 'I had such plans for you' — treating Richard like a promising student who has revealed fatal limitations
jokes.explanation: Six words and they're devastating. The pathos of Dan having had 'plans' for Richard — plans now ruined by Richard's inability to operate an on/off switch — is both funny and, in Veep's weird way, almost touching. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: Congressman Baxter, I completely believe you about the whole men's room incident. It sounded very plausible to me.
jokes.setup: Selina is working the party and encounters Congressman Baxter
jokes.punchline: A reference to a Larry Craig-style men's room scandal — Selina's overly earnest 'it sounded very plausible to me' is the sarcasm disguised as sincerity, or sincerity so extreme it reads as sarcasm
jokes.explanation: The 'men's room incident' implies a Larry Craig-type gay sex sting, and Selina's 'it sounded very plausible' is political schmoozing so intense it transcends irony. She doesn't care if he did it — she wants his vote. The joke is about how desperate she is and how transactions work in politics.

jokes.text: He could go either way. Like Baxter.
jokes.setup: Describing Congressman Paul Graves as a purple-state congressman who 'could go either way'
jokes.punchline: The aside 'Like Baxter' — connecting Graves' political ambiguity to Baxter's sexual ambiguity (the men's room incident) with the double meaning of 'go either way'
jokes.explanation: Three words — 'Like Baxter' — doing enormous work. The callback is economically perfect, relying on the audience to catch the double meaning of 'goes either way.' This is Veep's walk-and-talk patter at its most efficient.

jokes.text: Yeah, he wouldn't return my calls after POTUS fired him. Oh, come on. It's been a long time. Go talk to him.
jokes.setup: Mike sees his old work friend Jim Owens who hasn't returned his calls since being fired
jokes.punchline: Ben encourages Mike to go talk to him — the cringe of White House social dynamics where professional firing destroys personal relationships
jokes.explanation: This is more plot than comedy — establishing Mike's estranged work friend. Below average for the episode. The awkwardness has comedic intent but it's not executing a strong joke, more of a relatable social scenario setup.

jokes.text: Well, at least we know you don't need big balls. Got large stones for sure.
jokes.setup: Congressman Graves asks for Secretary of State in exchange for his vote
jokes.punchline: Selina's comment that he doesn't need 'big balls' (nerve/courage) to ask for State — followed by Ben's 'large stones for sure' — both wordplay on genitalia-as-audacity
jokes.explanation: Standard Veep wordplay in the political-negotiation register. The 'balls/stones' parallelism is functional but not particularly inventive. Ben's echo rather than escalation keeps it from being sharper.

jokes.text: Oh, what about Doyle? You promised him State. He knows I don't keep my promises. He should know by now.
jokes.setup: Selina has just promised Secretary of State to Graves to win his vote
jokes.punchline: Ben points out she already promised State to Doyle; Selina's response — 'He knows I don't keep my promises' — frames her political dishonesty as an established, understood relationship dynamic
jokes.explanation: 'He knows I don't keep my promises' is a great Selina line — her corruption isn't even hidden, it's just mutually understood. 'He should know by now' adds the perfect condescending topper, implying Doyle is naive if he believed her. Strong character integration: only Selina Meyer would say this about herself with zero shame.

jokes.text: And I said maybe you'd remember better if I fuck your brain through your ear hole.
jokes.setup: Furlong reports that Nickerson is 'back on the fence' and he tried to persuade her
jokes.punchline: Furlong's method of reminding Nickerson of her commitment — threatening to 'fuck her brain through her ear hole'
jokes.explanation: Furlong's insults are always anatomically creative. 'Fuck your brain through your ear hole' is visceral and specific. It's Furlong being Furlong — this is what congressional persuasion sounds like in his office. Character-specific to the show's most reliably foul character.

jokes.text: Let's say that your chief of staff... Nadia, is it? And she's down on her knees and she's got your balls in her hand and she's working your shaft just the way you like it...
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to explain abstention to Jaeger 'in terms he might understand'
jokes.punchline: Her chosen terms — a graphic sexual scenario involving his actual escort/staffer Nadia — is the setup; what follows escalates it further
jokes.explanation: The callback to Nadia being there makes this land much harder — Selina is using the actual sex worker as the character in her political analogy, which Jaeger must acknowledge. The mounting graphic detail as presidential political education is great cringe escalation.

jokes.text: ...she says, 'Nyet, after much prayerful consideration, I have to abstain from the upcoming blowjob.' Are you Nadia in this situation?
jokes.setup: Selina's extended sexual analogy for abstention from the vote
jokes.punchline: 'Nyet, after much prayerful consideration, I have to abstain from the upcoming blowjob' — mirroring Jaeger's exact language about prayerful abstention. The 'Are you Nadia?' closer reanchors it to the real sex worker in the room.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic peak. The structure is near-flawless: Selina takes Jaeger's pompous 'after much prayerful consideration I must abstain' language and transplants it word-for-word into a sexual context with his actual escort. 'Nyet' adds the Russian detail specific to Nadia's nationality. The closer — 'Are you Nadia in this situation?' — is a presidential demand that a congressman identify himself as the sex worker in a scenario Selina invented. Three callbacks merged into one escalation. This is what distinguishes Veep from any other political comedy.

jokes.text: Let me guess, Jaeger's abstaining. Yeah. Nickerson, too. Tom's not lobbying votes for O'Brien. He's asking people to abstain.
jokes.setup: Selina comes out of her confrontation with Jaeger
jokes.punchline: Ben has already figured out Jaeger is abstaining before Selina tells him — and the larger reveal is Tom's strategy, which is both more cunning and more demoralizing than simple vote-stealing
jokes.explanation: More plot exposition than comedy, though Ben's 'let me guess' is a good character beat showing he's three steps ahead. The comedic element is how quickly the crisis is processed — Veep's rhythm of disaster.

jokes.text: I've been watching you all night and I know what you're up to. Oh? You're trying to get up the courage to ask me to dance.
jokes.setup: Selina approaches Tom knowing he's been sabotaging her; Tom expects to be confronted about the abstention strategy
jokes.punchline: Selina pretends to accuse Tom of wanting to dance — deflecting the confrontation with a social misdirection that simultaneously puts Tom on his back foot and Selina on the dance floor where she can interrogate him privately
jokes.explanation: Strategically and comedically excellent. The tension of Selina approaching Tom — knowing he's been working against her — is built up, then released into 'you're trying to get up the courage to ask me to dance.' It's comedy and political maneuvering simultaneously. The surrounding crowd's applause for the dance then traps Tom perfectly.

jokes.text: I think all this meeting and greeting has really made me work up an appetite. I'm starving. Yeah, you never get the chance to eat at these things. Never. You know what I really have a hankering for? Korean barbecue. There's this great place I heard about.
jokes.setup: Selina and Tom are dancing; she leads him to believe they're having a casual food conversation
jokes.punchline: The pivot to 'Korean barbecue' — specifically the restaurant where Tom met with Marwood and Purcell — as a casual social observation. Tom doesn't know she knows.
jokes.explanation: Beautiful slow burn. Selina builds an entire food conversation — appetite, meeting and greeting, 'hankering' — before dropping 'Korean barbecue' as a bomb. The callback to the meeting location is deployed as a casual conversational topic. Structurally elegant and character-perfect — Selina at her most dangerous.

jokes.text: (WHISPERS) the Cocksucking Backstabber.
jokes.setup: On the dance floor, Selina mentions a Korean barbecue place and wonders what it's called; Tom is now visibly unnerved
jokes.punchline: Selina whispers the (fake) name of the restaurant — 'The Cocksucking Backstabber' — as if this is its actual name, publicly accusing Tom of betrayal while maintaining the appearance of dance-floor small talk
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's other peak moment alongside the Nadia blowjob abstention analogy. The structure is impeccable: the extended food-conversation setup makes the whispered 'Cocksucking Backstabber' land as both the restaurant's punchline name and a direct accusation. The instruction 'Oh, keep dancing, Tom. Don't stop dancing. Don't look around. Nobody's gonna help you.' delivered while continuing to dance publicly is Selina at her most theatrical and most menacing. Exceptional structure, economy, and character integration. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Oh, keep dancing, Tom. Don't stop dancing. Don't look around. Nobody's gonna help you.
jokes.setup: Tom is now trapped on the dance floor after Selina has whispered her accusation
jokes.punchline: Selina's choreographic commands — 'keep dancing,' 'don't stop,' 'nobody's gonna help you' — turn a dance into a hostage situation
jokes.explanation: Companion piece to the Korean barbecue/Cocksucking Backstabber line. 'Nobody's gonna help you' delivered while smiling at a White House ball is menacing, funny, and perfectly Selina. The contrast between the civil setting and the threat is the source of the comedy. This scene is peak Veep.

jokes.text: How stupid are you, you motherfucking snake? I know all about your meeting with Marwood and Purcell.
jokes.setup: The dance is over; Selina now confronts Tom privately
jokes.punchline: The transition from dance floor diplomacy to 'you motherfucking snake' in a private room — the pressure release after the sustained dance-floor tension
jokes.explanation: The comedic value here is in the tonal shift — from choreographic whisper-threats to open fury. 'Motherfucking snake' after the sustained elegance of the dance is the release valve. Not the sharpest line but earns its place in the sequence.

jokes.text: And, by the way, by messing around with that pretty boy Charlie Baird, for God's sake. What? Charlie Baird? What does Charlie Baird have to do with any of this? I don't know. He's got nothing to do with it.
jokes.setup: Selina is confronting Tom about his treachery; she throws in Charlie Baird as an accusation
jokes.punchline: Tom has no idea who Charlie Baird is; Selina's response — 'I don't know. He's got nothing to do with it.' — reveals she threw out a random name and is now defending her own mistake
jokes.explanation: Selina's tactical blunder of throwing a random name into her accusation — then admitting 'he's got nothing to do with it' — shows her losing control in the confrontation. Her inability to maintain the upper hand even in her own ambush is great character comedy. It also plants a running thread about Charlie Baird.

jokes.text: You are lying now just like you lied back then just like you lied about that night in the cab with the green shoes.
jokes.setup: Selina is accusing Tom of lying about his motivations for joining her ticket
jokes.punchline: The 'green shoes' — a specific, unexplained detail from a night in a cab that clearly has romantic significance — introduced as proof of a lie with no context provided to Tom or the audience
jokes.explanation: The 'green shoes' is an excellent piece of comedy writing — a specific, intimate detail that reveals a whole unexplored history between Selina and Tom. The fact that Tom then claims not to remember the shoes makes Selina's follow-up joke ('No straight man remembers a woman's shoes') even better. Character-revealing and economically deployed.

jokes.text: No straight man remembers a woman's shoes. Well, you got me. I'm gay, so...
jokes.setup: Selina argues Tom must have been attracted to her because 'no straight man remembers a woman's shoes'
jokes.punchline: Tom's deadpan 'Well, you got me. I'm gay, so...' — an absurd non-confession that deflates Selina's entire theory while also refusing to engage with it seriously
jokes.explanation: Tom's 'I'm gay, so...' is a perfect deadpan escape from Selina's trap — it acknowledges her premise (gay men notice shoes) while refusing to confirm any attraction. The trailing 'so...' leaves everything hanging. Tom wins the moment entirely. The delivery makes it work — it's dismissive, not defensive.

jokes.text: Come on, Tom. You wanted to fuck me that night. Just say it. You wanted to fuck my brains out. All right, I wanted to fuck your brains out! There you go. And now you're trying to fuck me tonight. No, I am fucking you tonight.
jokes.setup: The confrontation has devolved into Selina demanding Tom admit sexual attraction as some form of political victory
jokes.punchline: Tom finally breaks and admits it — then the word 'fuck' pivots from sexual to political betrayal in the same breath, and they argue over who is 'fucking' whom in the political sense
jokes.explanation: The escalation of 'fuck' from sexual to political is beautifully constructed. Tom's forced admission ('All right, I wanted to fuck your brains out!') should be the end — but Selina's pivot ('And now you're trying to fuck me tonight. No, I am fucking you tonight.') transforms the word's meaning in real time. The exchange ends in both of them claiming to be the fucker, which captures the entire power struggle. This is Veep at peak structural cleverness.

jokes.text: I mean, if I never see the White House again, it'll be too soon.
jokes.setup: Will is confiding to someone at the party, seemingly overheard
jokes.punchline: Will's garbled mixed idiom — 'if I never see X again, it'll be too soon' combines 'I hope I never see it again' with 'it's been too long' to produce the opposite of what he means
jokes.explanation: Will's malapropism — combining two idioms into a self-contradicting statement about the White House — is a small but well-crafted character beat. Consistent with Will as a character who sounds like he should make sense but doesn't. Good economy.

jokes.text: It's funny, you know. I love my job. I've been working for Congressman Furlong since I was 15.
jokes.setup: Will follows his self-contradicting White House complaint with a job satisfaction claim
jokes.punchline: The reveal that Will has worked for Furlong — the show's most verbally abusive character — since he was 15. This explains and contextualizes Will's damaged relationship with language and authority.
jokes.explanation: The dark comedy of Will having worked for Furlong since age 15 — being subjected to that level of verbal abuse as a teenager — recontextualizes every Will scene. 'I love my job' after that revelation is the punchline. Rewards viewers familiar with Furlong's treatment of Will.

jokes.text: Listen, I'm a high functioning alcoholic and you're a sloppy weekend drunk. Now get with it.
jokes.setup: Mike is visibly drunk at the party; Ben is confronting him
jokes.punchline: Ben's hierarchy — high-functioning alcoholic vs. sloppy weekend drunk — positions himself as the superior drunk and Mike as an amateur, treating alcoholism as a professional credential
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines. 'High functioning alcoholic' vs. 'sloppy weekend drunk' as a professional distinction — and Ben being proud of the former — is exceptional character work. 'Now get with it' as a managerial command to a fellow drunk is the perfect closer. This line could be pulled out of context and work as a standalone joke. Near-perfect economy, originality, and character integration.

jokes.text: (MOANING) [Selina and Tom apparently having sex, discovered by an aide]
jokes.setup: An aide knocks on the door looking for the president mid-crisis; earlier in the scene Selina and Tom's confrontation had a charged sexual dimension
jokes.punchline: The aide finds Selina and Tom mid-coitus — the entire political conflict has somehow resolved into sex, confirmed by moaning sounds and shaky breathing, making the 'I am fucking you tonight' line a literal double entendre
jokes.explanation: The entire Tom-Selina confrontation arc pays off in the best possible way: the argument about who is fucking whom becomes literal. Every layer of the scene — the dance, the shoes, the 'brains out' admission — is retroactively the setup for this moment. The shaky breathing and whimpers heard through the door are perfect comedy beats. The aide just says 'Ma'am?' into the void. This is structurally one of the best jokes in the episode — every prior beat was its setup.

jokes.text: Hi. Jonah Ryan. Thanks for coming out tonight. Let me know if you have any questions. Hi. Jonah Ryan. Running for Congress and I hope I can count on your support.
jokes.setup: Cut to Jonah at a bowling alley doing retail politics
jokes.punchline: Jonah treating a bowling alley like a campaign event — 'thanks for coming out tonight' as if he's running the venue — is an absurdist application of politician mode to an entirely inappropriate setting
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of campaign-mode Jonah in a bowling alley is inherently funny but the specific lines are generic politician patter. Works more as setup for the subsequent jokes.

jokes.text: Big shoes. You know what that means, right? Uh, big feet? Can't argue with that. Maybe you should run for Congress.
jokes.setup: Jonah tries a classic 'big shoes means big... [implied genitalia]' joke with a bowling alley worker
jokes.punchline: The worker's literal answer — 'big feet?' — takes the wind out of Jonah's innuendo; Jonah's response 'can't argue with that, maybe you should run for Congress' is both a recovery and an inadvertent endorsement of literal thinking as a political qualification
jokes.explanation: The setup/punchline inversion works nicely — Jonah's innuendo gets literally deflated. But 'Maybe you should run for Congress' as a response to literal-minded thinking is the real joke, implying Congress is full of people who don't understand what's being implied. Good structure.

jokes.text: So why does your shirt say Jon H Ryan?
jokes.setup: Jonah has introduced himself as Jonah Ryan; the bowling alley worker looks at his campaign shirt
jokes.punchline: The shirt says 'Jon H Ryan' — a campaign merchandise error that literally misspells the candidate's name at his own campaign event
jokes.explanation: A perfect Jonah campaign detail — his own shirt can't spell his name. 'Fuck.' is the only possible response and it's the right one. The joke is in the specificity: Jon H Ryan suggests someone split JONAH into JON H as if it were a middle initial. The error is both plausible and absurd. High economy.

jokes.text: Oh, bowling alley's always a good choice. I mean, skating rink's good for a male candidate, but it's cold and gals tend to nip out.
jokes.setup: Dan and Jonah are assessing the bowling alley as a campaign event venue
jokes.punchline: 'Gals tend to nip out' — Dan's campaign strategy advice segues into an observation about women's nipples in cold environments as a reason to avoid skating rinks
jokes.explanation: Dan's campaign advice pivoting from legitimate strategy ('skating rink is good for a male candidate') to 'gals tend to nip out' as a venue selection criterion is character-perfect — his entire political worldview filtered through objectification. The fact that it's delivered as genuine professional wisdom makes it funnier.

jokes.text: What are you doing here, Teddy? I'm looking forward to some bowling. Although pocket pool is more my game.
jokes.setup: Teddy — established as a sexual abuser of Jonah in prior episodes — appears at the bowling alley
jokes.punchline: 'Pocket pool is more my game' — Teddy's coded reference to groping (a real euphemism for touching oneself in one's pocket) in response to being at a bowling alley, delivered as innocent conversation while Jonah understands the threat
jokes.explanation: Teddy's 'pocket pool' is an actual billiards term and slang — the dual meaning works because Teddy's character history makes the innocent reading impossible. The nonchalance of the delivery at a bowling alley is perfect. Dark comedy with real stakes given Teddy's history as an abuser.

jokes.text: I was a victim, but I am not gonna let you define me. Nice blog, Mary.
jokes.setup: Jonah confronts Teddy, who has been sexually abusing him
jokes.punchline: Jonah's genuine 'I was a victim, but I am not gonna let you define me' is a serious assertion of abuse survival — immediately undercut by 'Nice blog, Mary,' a mocking aside that destroys his moment of dignity
jokes.explanation: 'Nice blog, Mary' directed at Teddy undercuts Jonah's empowerment speech — it's a sudden pivot from genuine-feeling survivor speech to petty school-yard mockery. The whiplash is the joke. 'Mary' as a feminizing taunt while accusing someone of being a predator is dark but character-consistent with Jonah.

jokes.text: You know what? People like you, you look like us, but there's a little piece missing. You're like a different species and one that I hope gets struck from the Earth because you're a bully and Jonah Ryan stands up to bullies.
jokes.setup: Jonah is confronting Teddy; this speech is being filmed by local news
jokes.punchline: The speech is sincere but Jonah, who is himself the most prominent bully in the show, is claiming to stand up to bullies. The irony is total. Additionally, this is being filmed and will be used against him out of context.
jokes.explanation: The dramatic irony — Jonah calling someone else a bully — is the engine. And the scene pays off structurally when the footage appears on the news stripped of Teddy, making Jonah look like he's attacking a random woman at a bowling alley. The 'different species' language is exactly what gets clipped.

jokes.text: Okay, you and Boo-Boo the ball-fondling bear here have exactly two seconds to get the fuck out.
jokes.setup: Dan moves to eject Teddy from the event
jokes.punchline: 'Boo-Boo the ball-fondling bear' — Dan's insult for Teddy, referencing the Yogi Bear sidekick while incorporating the groping accusation
jokes.explanation: 'Boo-Boo the ball-fondling bear' is an exceptional insult — the Yogi Bear reference is unexpected, the 'ball-fondling' is specific to Teddy's offense, and 'bear' adds a third layer. This is Veep insult craft at a very high level. The specificity and the cultural reference working together is what elevates it above a generic epithet.

jokes.text: Our work is done here. Tell Selina hello. Yeah, great place for an event, Dan. The teenager selling cocaine in the bathroom thought so, too.
jokes.setup: Teddy leaves with a parting shot about the venue choice
jokes.punchline: Dan's response validating the cocaine-dealing teenager as a positive venue endorsement — treating a drug dealer's choice of workplace as a Yelp review
jokes.explanation: Dan's decision to treat the cocaine-dealing teenager as venue validation is perfectly Dan — he finds the angle that wins even in the worst situation. The specificity of 'teenager selling cocaine in the bathroom' as a non-sequitur reveal about the venue is great. Economy is strong.

jokes.text: Hey. — Oh, looking good, 26.
jokes.setup: Dan and Amy encounter each other
jokes.punchline: Amy calls Dan by his hot list ranking number rather than his name — simultaneously dismissing him and proving she knows exactly where he ranked
jokes.explanation: A sharp callback. Amy demonstrates that she has memorized everyone's rankings despite claiming not to care. 'Oh, looking good, 26' is good economy — three words that do the work of 'I know your number, I've been tracking it, and I'm using it to diminish you.'

jokes.text: It's where Woodrow Wilson had some of his most significant strokes.
jokes.setup: Selina invites Congresswoman Nickerson to the Green Room by saying it's 'historic'
jokes.punchline: Describing the Green Room as notable for Woodrow Wilson's strokes — meaning both 'strokes of genius' in political deals and literal medical strokes (Wilson did have incapacitating strokes)
jokes.explanation: The double meaning of 'significant strokes' — political/intellectual strokes versus the cerebrovascular events that actually incapacitated Wilson — is historically accurate and genuinely clever. Selina says it as a compliment/sales pitch, but the literal reading is that the Green Room is where Wilson's brain started failing. Excellent wordplay with real historical grounding. High economy and originality.

jokes.text: You got the hockey job. Perfect. Where are you headed? I'm going to CL International Time. That sounds great. Mmm, big.
jokes.setup: Mike and Jim reconnect; Jim has a new job
jokes.punchline: 'CL International Time' — Jim's new job at an organization whose name sounds prestigious but makes no sense. Mike's 'That sounds great. Mmm, big.' endorses something neither of them seems to fully understand.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the institutional name 'CL International Time' — which sounds like a real organization but is clearly gibberish — and Mike's unquestioning endorsement. Both of them perform enthusiasm for something neither understands. Washington culture captured in miniature.

jokes.text: If you think that you are getting those worthless toy boats now, I'm gonna shut down those factories faster than you can bedazzle a fucking sweatshirt.
jokes.setup: Selina turns on Congresswoman Nickerson who has been stalling her vote
jokes.punchline: 'Faster than you can bedazzle a fucking sweatshirt' — targeting Nickerson's suburban-mom aesthetic as a speed metaphor for her revenge
jokes.explanation: The callback to Nickerson's suburban mom identity — now weaponized as a simile for speed — is elegant. 'Bedazzle a fucking sweatshirt' is a specifically perfect image of Nickerson's world used as a measuring stick for presidential revenge speed. 'Worthless toy boats' for the amphibious fighting boat is also a good dismissal.

jokes.text: You're playing a very dangerous game of chicken with the head fucking hen.
jokes.setup: Selina warns Nickerson about the political consequences of not supporting her
jokes.punchline: 'Head fucking hen' — Selina refers to herself as the 'head hen' (top of the pecking order) in a game of chicken — mixing metaphors but with manic conviction
jokes.explanation: 'Head fucking hen' is a great Selina invention — she's trying to say 'I am top chicken in this game of chicken' and what she produces is a mixed metaphor that somehow still lands. The aggression overwhelms the incoherence.

jokes.text: O'Brien is gonna sink your stupid boats and you're gonna look like a hair-sprayed asshole in your 1980s mother-of-the-bride dress.
jokes.setup: Selina continues threatening Nickerson with political consequences if O'Brien wins
jokes.punchline: The specific insult — '1980s mother-of-the-bride dress' and 'hair-sprayed asshole' — returns to Nickerson's appearance as a suburban political relic
jokes.explanation: 'Hair-sprayed asshole in your 1980s mother-of-the-bride dress' is a well-targeted insult — specific to Nickerson's implied aesthetic in a way that 'you'll look bad' is not. The image is precise and funny. Part of an extended threat sequence that escalates effectively.

jokes.text: And then I'm gonna have the IRS crawl so far up your husband's colon, he's gonna wish the only thing they find is more cancer.
jokes.setup: Selina's threat to Nickerson escalates from political to personal
jokes.punchline: The IRS audit of the husband who is currently in chemotherapy — 'he's gonna wish the only thing they find is more cancer' — treating cancer as preferable to an IRS audit
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's most technically accomplished joke. It's a three-episode callback to the opening scene (Donald has chemo today / Selina: 'Fantastic!'). Now Selina weaponizes that cancer against Nickerson in a threat so dark it circles back to comedy — 'he's gonna wish the only thing they find is more cancer' treats cancer as the lesser evil to IRS scrutiny. The structure (IRS crawling up his colon → cancer as relief) is shocking and precise. This is the caliber of Veep insult writing that defines the show.

jokes.text: So can I count on your vote? Or do I need to shove a box of White House M&Ms up your stretched-out, six-baby vag?
jokes.setup: After the extended threat sequence, Selina closes with her vote ask
jokes.punchline: The alternative to voting yes is 'shove a box of White House M&Ms up your stretched-out, six-baby vag' — deploying the White House gift item from scene 1 as a threatening object against Nickerson's body
jokes.explanation: A masterclass callback. In the opening scene: (1) Selina offers gift packages including mugs/blankets — now M&Ms, and (2) Nickerson mentions being a 'stay-at-home mother of six.' Both details return as the punchline of a presidential threat to violate a congresswoman with a branded candy box. The 'six-baby vag' is anatomically specific to the setup. The transformation of diplomatic gift into threat and the use of 'six children' against the mother is structurally brilliant. One of the best callbacks in the episode.

jokes.text: I think I want to hear an okey-dokey, Annie Oakley. Okey-dokey, Annie Oakley. Oh, super-duper, trooper. Now get the fuck out of here, Congresswoman.
jokes.setup: Nickerson has just agreed to vote for Selina after the extended threat
jokes.punchline: Selina demands Nickerson use her own folksy catchphrase — 'okey-dokey, Annie Oakley' — as a verbal confirmation of capitulation; then adds her own 'super-duper, trooper' before dismissing her
jokes.explanation: Perfect structural callback. 'Okey-dokey, Annie Oakley' was Nickerson's charming folksy agreement in scene 1 — now Selina weaponizes it as a ritual of submission. Forcing Nickerson to repeat her own cutesy phrase back as a surrender oath, then capping it with 'super-duper, trooper' in the same register, is a complete arc. The folksy language as coercion is dark and funny. Excellent structure.

jokes.text: 'Nev-add-a.' I told POTUS that there are other qualified people out there for special advisor, but she insisted she couldn't think of any, so...
jokes.setup: Amy runs into Candi Caruso, who is at the party as Selina's special advisor
jokes.punchline: Candi corrects Amy's Nevada pronunciation then reveals she got her job because Selina 'couldn't think of anyone' else — an unintentional self-own disguised as a humblebrag
jokes.explanation: The pronunciation correction is a character note (Candi is pedantic/showy). The 'she insisted she couldn't think of any' is the real joke — Candi thinks she's relating a compliment but is actually revealing she's a last resort. Functional.

jokes.text: I am engaged. Mm-hmm. Who's... to whom? Gary Welsh. You know, Gary Welsh. Gary Welsh? Oh! Gary Welsh at State. He was supposed to be on this 50 Hottest thing that I'm in, but they got his name wrong.
jokes.setup: Candi reveals she's engaged; Amy asks to whom
jokes.punchline: Candi is engaged to Gary Welsh at State — NOT Gary Walsh (Selina's bag man from the hot list). Gary Walsh is on the hot list due to a name misspelling of a completely different person.
jokes.explanation: The Gary hot list running gag's best payoff — Gary Walsh has been receiving celebrity treatment all episode due to a name misspelling. The reveal that the actual Gary Welsh is engaged to Candi Caruso recontextualizes every Gary hot list moment. Excellent structural callback — the audience gets the joke before Amy or Gary Walsh does.

jokes.text: It wasn't you. What? You're not hot. Gary Welsh at State is. You're a typo.
jokes.setup: Gary Walsh believes he's been on the hot list all episode; Amy has just learned the truth
jokes.punchline: Amy delivers the devastating news: 'You're a typo.' — reducing Gary's entire episode-long celebrity to a clerical error
jokes.explanation: 'You're a typo' is one of the episode's best lines and the perfect payoff to the Gary hot list running gag. Three words that devastate an episode-long character journey. The specificity — not 'you're wrong' or 'it was a mistake' but 'you're a typo' — transforms Gary from a person into a spelling error. Peak economy. The contrast with 'but like you said, it's not up here, it's what's in here' (Dan's earlier pep talk) is a dark callback. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: But like you said, it's not up here, it's what's in here.
jokes.setup: Amy has just told Gary he's a typo; she continues
jokes.punchline: Amy recycles Dan's earlier inner-beauty pep talk — which was delivered to HER as condescension — and uses it to comfort Gary, who is now in her exact previous position
jokes.explanation: A clean structural callback — Dan's hollow pep talk to Amy is now recycled by Amy as an equally hollow comfort to Gary. The same patronizing advice passes down the hierarchy of wounded pride. The irony that Amy (not on the list) is comforting Gary (a typo) with consolation she knows is empty is dark and funny. Well-constructed.

jokes.text: Fuck me, Amadeus. Can't even see the guy that Jonah's yelling at. God, Polly's not gonna think that I was yelling at her, will she?
jokes.setup: News footage of Jonah's confrontation with Teddy plays on TV — but Teddy can't be seen in the footage, making it look like Jonah yelled at a random person
jokes.punchline: Jonah's concern — 'Polly's not gonna think I was yelling at her?' — reveals his primary concern is not the political damage but whether Polly (the cute bowling alley worker) thinks badly of him
jokes.explanation: 'Fuck me, Amadeus' is a good oath. But the real comedy is Jonah's concern about Polly rather than the electoral disaster — his personal vanity trumping political self-preservation is pure Jonah. The 'will she?' as a genuine question about the cute bowling alley worker is great character comedy.

jokes.text: Wait a second. Richard, tell me you have that from the other angle. Oh, definitely. [FOOTAGE: Teddy shown] What are you doing here? You did the same fucking thing! Literally. Oh, yeah, you know what? You're right, I did the exact same thing.
jokes.setup: The news footage is a disaster because Teddy can't be seen; Dan realizes Richard was filming from another angle
jokes.punchline: Richard's footage shows TEDDY clearly — but Richard's footage is also of Richard doing the exact same camera confusion (turning it off when it should be on) — the exculpatory footage is meta-evidence of the same failure
jokes.explanation: The final callback to Richard's camera incompetence is structurally brilliant — the footage that could have saved Jonah is footage of Richard making the exact same mistake that cost them the original footage. The echo structure ('you did the same fucking thing!' / 'Literally.' / 'you're right, I did') is a miniature comedic callback loop. Richard confirming it with 'oh, definitely' before revealing the footage is the timing detail that makes it land.

jokes.text: After the national elections, the president may have found herself with a bad lie in the rough, but after stabilizing the economy, the House vote is starting to look like a gimme putt. And mark my words, the president will end up atop the leaderboard just like Inbee Park or Lydia Ko or any of the other great female golfers in the LPGA Tour.
jokes.setup: Mike is doing a press briefing; he has been using sports metaphors throughout
jokes.punchline: Mike's final press briefing is entirely in golf metaphors — 'bad lie in the rough,' 'gimme putt,' 'leaderboard,' name-dropping specific LPGA players — as if he's switched sports from hockey to golf, or is trying to cover his tracks
jokes.explanation: Mike has switched from hockey to golf metaphors — possibly because Selina called him out on the hockey ones. The specificity of naming Inbee Park and Lydia Ko (actual LPGA players) adds authenticity to the absurdity. The running gag gets a final iteration that confirms Mike's compulsive sports metaphor problem is not sport-specific.

jokes.text: Reporter at Kent's press conference: 'I would like to get back to questions about the first daughter's lesbianism.'
jokes.setup: Kent has just done an elaborate golf metaphor press conference
jokes.punchline: The reporter ignores all the sports metaphor deflections and simply re-states their question — the persistence of the Catherine story despite all the PR management
jokes.explanation: A good closing beat — all the elaborate Catherine management and press deflection has accomplished nothing. The word 'lesbianism' delivered flatly is slightly incongruous and funny as an episode-closing word.

jokes.text: I would like to get back to questions about the first daughter's lesbianism.
jokes.setup: Mike has just concluded a golf-metaphor-heavy briefing summary; a reporter immediately redirects
jokes.punchline: The reporter cuts through the entire sports metaphor absurdity to ask the question everyone actually wants answered — and the word 'lesbianism' delivered in a bored press briefing voice is the comedy
jokes.explanation: The journalist cutting through Mike's extended metaphor to return to what everyone wants is a good structural callback to the opening press briefing (where Mike dodged the Catherine question with hockey buzzers). The flat delivery of 'first daughter's lesbianism' in standard press briefing register is the comedy — it's both earnest and clinical.

tmdb_overview: At the White House Christmas party, Selina bargains for votes in hopes of breaking the election tie. Meanwhile, the staff is preoccupied with the annual '50 Hottest D.C. Staffers' list; Mike eyes a job outside politics; and Dan helps Jonah with debate prep in New Hampshire.
insight: Cringe escalation sustains 79 jokes across a single congressional event with relentless character chaos.
